dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a call of chest pain at the given address in Richmond.
Hi, I'm Alex. I'm an EMT with the rescue squad. What brought you to call 911 today?
I'm experiencing a 10/10 sharp and stabbing chest pain on the left side. It's been on and off since Saturday.
Are you able to tell me your name, where you are, what the date is, and what's happening to you right now?
My name is Ruth. I'm at my home in Richmond. It is December 2019. I'm having chest pain and called 911.
You are alert and oriented to person, place, time, and event. GCS is 15. Pupils are equal at 3 mm and reactive.
Can you move your right arm? Left arm? Right leg? Left leg? Let's check your motor function.
Yes, I can move them. Though I have some weakness on my right side because of a stroke I had in June.
Your airway is patent. No signs of obstruction. Your jaw is aligned and there are no abnormal sounds.
You're breathing at 16 breaths per minute. Equal, unlabored chest rise and fall. No flaring or retractions.
Pulse is 79 and regular, blood pressure is 169 over 104. Skin is warm, dry, with strong peripheral pulses.
Did you perform any physical activity, eat, or drink prior to the chest pain starting?
I was just talking on the phone, sitting on the edge of the bed. I hadn't done anything that would provoke it.
Have you ever had chest pain like this before, and was there ever any heart condition or infarct?
I haven't had a heart attack, but I did have a stroke. I've never had chest pain this sharp before.
What medications are you currently taking, and how are you handling your blood sugar as an insulin-dependent person?
I take Apidra for diabetes, lisinopril, carvedilol, tramadol, gabapentin, and some other tablets for anxiety and sleep.
Have you had any allergic reactions or adverse responses to medications in the past?
No, I don't have any drug allergies that I'm aware of.
You mentioned a stroke in June and a pacemaker. Can you tell me more about those?
Stroke left some weakness on my right side. The pacemaker was inserted at VCU, three to four weeks ago.
Your glucose level is 204 mg/dL. That is high for someone with insulin-dependent diabetes. Did you eat before that?
Your oxygen saturation is 99% on room air. That means you are receiving sufficient oxygen.
Your EKG at 16:17 shows sinus rhythm. No acute distress visible at this time. Just to clarify it further, we'll do a 12-lead.
Listening to your lungs-equal breath sounds in both fields, no wheezes or crackles.
Heart sounds are normal. No murmur, rub, or gallop noted. No signs of tachycardia or arrhythmia.
Your abdomen is soft, non-tender, without distention or guarding. Bowel sounds are not present, but that's normal without auscultation.
I started a 20-gauge IV in your left antecubital with normal saline running at a KVO rate.
I gave you 243 mg of aspirin. You told me you had taken 81 mg before we arrived, so I administered the remainder.
We have initiated 4-lead continuous monitoring and obtained a 12-lead EKG at 16:22. It showed abnormalities of concern.
Your skin has normal temperature, color, and turgor. No signs of rashes, bruises, or trauma.
No swelling, lacerations, or bites on your head or neck. Pupils are still equal and reactive.
No drainage from nostrils, ears, or mouth. No throat swelling or foreign bodies noted.
Your chest is not deformed. No paradoxical movement or signs of contributory fracture. Good airflow.
You can move both arms and legs. No rigidness or swelling noted in the extremities. No deformity or angulation.
When did the sharp and stabbing pain start exactly? Was it sudden or did it build up gradually?
It came all at once in a sharp stab. I didn't do anything to provoke it-it just happened.
And how long was the pain present at its peak before subsiding? What quality did you describe it as?
Each episode lasts a few seconds. It was sharp, like a knife moving across my chest. Two episodes so far.
You were assisted into a semi-Fowler's position and secured on the stretcher with straps and rails raised.
Hospital staff were called en route and made aware that we were transporting you as a hot patient to VCU.
Oxygen saturation is currently 98%, and you are not in respiratory distress. Supplemental oxygen is not needed.
Your breathing rate is now 18 breaths per minute, still regular, and effort appears not distressing.
A second 12-lead at 16:45 showed a possible anterior and inferior myocardial infarct. Results are consistent and transmitted.
The stabbing pain has significantly reduced since we started. Now you just feel mild discomfort. Does that align with your current condition?
Yes, the pain stopped after a few minutes in the ambulance. I just have residual mild discomfort.
Did any positions, medications, or other activities alleviate the pain or make it worse earlier today?
Nothing worked. It just went after its own time. I didn't try anything because it was brief.
EKG now shows sinus rhythm with no acute changes. VCU received the transmitted 12-lead EKG at 16:45.
Pulse is now 87 and still regular. Blood pressure is unchanged at 169/80 and respirations are 18.
VCU was updated on the EKG findings and on your medical history. You are set for direct admission to the ED.
You were safely transferred to the ED bed with rails raised and secured via draw sheet. No incident reported.
Care was passed to the triage nurse at Bed 12. Report and transfer were documented by a signature.
Per protocol 2-1 (medical - chest pain - cardiac suspected), we initiated EKG, IV fluid, and aspirin in line with field assessment and med history.
